author: Dr. Jennifer Adams
content:
Telephone encounter with patient Robert Morrison regarding follow-up on recent office visit and medication questions.

REASON FOR CALL: Patient calling to follow up on referrals discussed at his 3/10/2025 PCP visit. Also has questions about his medication schedule.

REFERRAL STATUS:
- Ophthalmology (Dr. Karen Liu): Patient confirmed he completed his annual diabetic eye exam in December 2024. Results showed mild NPDR — he is aware and scheduled for 6-month follow-up in June 2025.
- Podiatry (Dr. Michael Chen): Completed December 2024. No acute foot concerns at that time.
- Cardiology (Dr. James Wilson): Next appointment scheduled for April 2025. Patient reports no change in cardiac symptoms — no increased dyspnea, no chest pain, no worsening edema. Weight stable 96-97 kg.
- Behavioral health: Patient states he has been considering the referral but has not yet scheduled. He reports mood is "about the same" — some days better than others. Sleeping OK. Appetite fair. Denies suicidal ideation. Encouraged him to schedule the intake — explained that counseling can help with adjustment to chronic illness burden. He agreed to call this week.

MEDICATION QUESTIONS:
- Patient confused about his furosemide dosing — taking 60mg in the AM and 40mg in the PM but wasn't sure if he should take the PM dose with lunch or dinner. Clarified: take PM dose in early afternoon (before 3 PM) to minimize nocturia, as documented in his medication instructions from Dr. Wilson.
- Asked about his gabapentin timing — currently taking 300mg three times daily but sometimes forgets the midday dose. Advised that consistent TID dosing is important for neuropathy symptom control. Suggested setting a phone alarm for the midday dose.
- Confirmed he is checking blood sugars as directed: fasting and before dinner. Reports fasting sugars 130-160 range, pre-dinner 140-180. A1c trending down (7.4% most recently) — encouraged by the improvement.

ADDITIONAL CONCERNS: Patient mentioned he has been having some increased urinary frequency at night (3-4 times). This is likely multifactorial — diuretic timing, BPH (on tamsulosin), and diabetes. Advised monitoring and will address at next office visit. If symptoms worsen significantly or he develops dysuria/hematuria, should call sooner.

PLAN:
1. Continue current medications as prescribed.
2. Patient to schedule behavioral health intake this week.
3. Address nocturia at next PCP visit (May 2025).
4. Cardiology follow-up April 2025.
5. Ophthalmology 6-month follow-up June 2025.
6. Next PCP visit already scheduled.

Total call duration: 14 minutes.
attestation:
Telephone encounter documented.

Electronically signed by: Dr. Jennifer Adams
Date: 3/20/2025
